system: You are a skeptical reviewer checking a single discovery finding before it reaches a client. You are given the finding and ONLY the evidence it cites (computed numbers + document quotes). Judge whether the stated conclusion is genuinely SUPPORTED by that evidence, or whether it over-reaches — e.g. asserts a causal link from correlational data, generalises beyond what the numbers show, or states something the quotes do not actually say. Default to skepticism: if the evidence does not clearly support the conclusion, mark it unsupported. Reply with strict JSON only: {"supported": true|false, "reason": "...", "suggested_fix": "..."}. suggested_fix is how to reword the finding to match only what the evidence supports (empty if supported).
prompt:
Review this finding:

TITLE: EDI is 67% of order volume (€59.7M) but is explicitly excluded from both the Order Management SOP and the O2C RACI — no documented owner for two-thirds of the business
CLAIM: The order-flow export shows EDI is the dominant channel: 5,667 of 8,420 orders (67.3% by count) worth €59,711,399 (66.8% of value). Yet the Order Management SOP explicitly states EDI 'is not covered by this version of the SOP', and the O2C RACI covers 'Manual (telephone) and Email order channels only', with EDI process steps 'not included in this version of the RACI'. The only EDI procedure is an informal, non-authoritative working note ('not an official SOP') maintained by the CS Lead. The consequence is visible in the escalation log: 'EDI order not processed — manual intervention required' is the single largest root cause at 34 of 142 cases (23.9%), all on the EDI channel — a failure mode the official documentation assigns to no owner.
BUSINESS CONSEQUENCE: Two-thirds of order volume (€59.7M) runs through a channel with no authoritative SOP and no RACI accountability; the most frequent service failure (EDI orders requiring manual re-entry) has no documented owner, leaving resolution dependent on informal working notes and risking systematic late delivery and customer delisting threats.
COMPUTED EVIDENCE: EDI share of orders by count (pct)=67.3; EDI order value (EUR)=59711399.53; EDI share of order value (pct)=66.8; Escalations: 'EDI order not processed — manual intervention required'=34; That root cause as pct of all escalations=23.9
DOCUMENT EVIDENCE: SOP excludes EDI: “Electronic Data Interchange (EDI) channel operations. EDI is not covered by this version of the SOP. EDI”; RACI covers only manual/email: “This RACI covers Manual (telephone) and Email order channels only. EDI channel”; No RACI row for EDI: “- A RACI for EDI dispute resolution (there is no row in the O2C RACI for EDI).”; EDI ~two-thirds of volume per CS notes: “purchase orders automatically — it accounts for around 67% of our total order volume.”
response: {"supported": true, "reason": "Every quantitative claim is backed by computed evidence (67.3% by count, \u20ac59.7M, 66.8% by value, 34/142=23.9% escalations) and every documentary claim is backed by direct quotes (SOP excludes EDI, RACI covers only manual/email, no EDI row, working note non-authoritative). The conclusion that a high-volume channel lacks authoritative SOP/RACI ownership follows directly. The business consequence about late delivery/delisting is framed as a risk ('risking'), not an asserted causal outcome, which is appropriate given the escalation log shows EDI-processing failures are the top root cause.", "suggested_fix": ""}
